name: Two-Mile Crib (Chicago Avenue Crib)
note: Pentagonal timber crib; fed the original 1867 Chicago Ave. lake tunnel. Closed and demolished 1936. Coordinates from Wikipedia's crib-inventory coord template (converted DMS); flagged approx per project convention for demolished cribs.

name: Four-Mile Crib
note: Masonry crib w/ lighthouse, designed by D.H. Hayes; fed Harrison St. pumping station. Still standing, mothballed; demolition funded 2015-17 but never executed. Exact deactivation date undocumented -- activeTo 1935 is an inference (roughly when the Harrison/Dever tunnel system reorganized North Side supply), not a directly sourced date.

name: 68th Street Crib
note: Paired with the Dunne Crib 50 ft away (steel footbridge); position offset slightly from Dunne's coordinate for map clarity, both drawn from the same Wikipedia geo-template. Standing, inactive, largely superseded once the Sawyer plant's own intakes came online. activeTo 1947 (Sawyer/South Water Filtration Plant opening) is an inference, not an explicitly documented deactivation date.

name: Lake View Crib
note: First Chicago water tunnel driven partly through rock. Superseded 1918 by the new Clarendon Ave. tunnel, intake shaft domed over 1922, crib demolished 1924. Coordinates from Wikipedia's crib coord template; flagged approx per project convention for demolished cribs.

name: Carter H. Harrison Crib
note: Stone crib faced with hydraulic pressed brick; replaced the Two-Mile Crib on the Chicago Ave. tunnels. Paired with the Dever Crib ~alongside it (coordinate offset slightly from Dever's for map clarity). Unusable since the 1997-98 tunnel collapse/dewatering-under-Lake-Shore-Drive fill.

name: Edward F. Dunne Crib
note: Circular crib, 50 ft from the 68th St. Crib (steel footbridge between). Active -- one of only two cribs still in service, supplying the Sawyer plant. City engineering records (1933/1956) instead date it 1911; both figures reported in the source doc. Coordinate offset slightly from the shared Wikipedia geo-template position (identical to 68th St. Crib) for map clarity.

name: Lawrence Avenue Crib
note: Small nearshore intake off the former Montrose Blvd. Abandoned; precise deactivation date, dimensions, and current physical status undocumented in sources reviewed (a 1913-catalogued CHM photo may push the true origin two years earlier than the 1915 date used here).

name: Wilson Avenue Crib
note: Steel-caisson-base/granite-block crib completed May 1, 1918 after a mid-construction re-leveling episode; fed ~8 miles of hand-dug rock tunnels to the Mayfair pumping station. Standing, inactive; now a cormorant/peregrine nesting site. Exact deactivation date undocumented.

name: William E. Dever Crib
note: Steel-and-concrete crib adjacent to the Harrison Crib; connected to the 16-ft, 11.5-mi Chicago Ave. lake tunnel (1936). Active -- one of only two cribs still in service, supplying the Jardine plant. Coordinate offset slightly from the shared Wikipedia geo-template position (identical to Harrison Crib) for map clarity.

name: Intermediate Crib (temporary construction crib)
note: Temporary wooden crib on the Dunne-tunnel construction route, ~7,500 ft from shore per Wikipedia (contemporary press said ~2 mi); site of the Jan. 20, 1909 fire that killed an estimated 47-70 tunnel workers. Removed after the fire. Position computed by interpolating along the shore-to-Dunne-Crib bearing at the documented 7,500 ft distance -- no direct coordinate exists in any source found.

name: James W. Jardine Water Purification Plant
note: 1000 E. Ohio St., north of Navy Pier. World's largest conventional filtration plant, 1.4 BGD rated capacity; approved as "Central District Filtration Plant," full operation 1964 (sometimes cited 1968), renamed 1977 for Commissioner James W. Jardine. Fed by the Harrison/Dever crib complex.

name: Eugene Sawyer Water Purification Plant
note: 3300 E. Cheltenham Pl., 79th St. area. Opened 1947 as "South Water Filtration Plant" (world's largest on opening), capacity expanded 1964, renamed 2016 for Mayor Eugene Sawyer. Fed by the Dunne/68th St. crib complex. Serves the south side, 720 MGD rated.

name: Chicago Avenue Pumping Station
note: 811 N. Michigan Ave. Oldest continuously operating station (site used since 1854's "Old Sally" engine); castellated-Gothic building by W.W. Boyington, paired with the Chicago Water Tower. Converted steam-to-electric in the 1930s; still active, now fed from the Jardine plant.

name: Central Park Pumping Station
note: 1015 S. Central Park Ave. Built 1899-1900 at the terminus of the Carter H. Harrison crib's northwest land tunnel. Underwent a ~$115M steam-to-electric VFD conversion, design ~2015, construction 2018-2025.

name: Springfield Pumping Station
note: 1747 N. Springfield Ave. Built 1901 alongside Central Park at the Harrison crib's land-tunnel terminus. Converted steam-to-electric under Mayor Emanuel, unveiled Dec. 2017 (~70% carbon cut, solar panels, green roof).

name: Mayfair Pumping Station
note: 4850 W. Wilson Ave. Opened 1918 (city architect Charles Kallal) at the end of an 8-mile hand-dug rock tunnel from the Wilson Ave. crib, ending decades of low North Side water pressure. In design phase for capital improvements as of the 2022-26 CIP.

name: Western Avenue Pumping Station
note: 4933 S. Western Ave. Opened Aug. 1927 -- on completion, "the largest municipally owned water pumping station in the world," 300 MGD rated. In design phase for capital improvements as of the 2022-26 CIP; steam-to-electric conversion queued for 2027-2030 construction.

name: Thomas Jefferson Pumping Station
note: 2250 W. Eastwood Ave. Built in under a year (architect Argyle Robinson); newest of the "classic 12" stations as of the 1933 official history. Received federal STAG grant funding (~$485,000) for improvements 2009-11.

name: Roseland Pumping Station
note: 351 W. 104th St. Served the newly tunneled southwest side via the Southwest Land and Connecting Tunnel from the Dunne crib. VFD replacement program funded 2009-13; suffered a high-pressure main leak/boil-water order July 2024.

name: 68th Street Pumping Station
note: 6801 S. Oglesby Ave. Site in use since 1868/1889, rebuilt early 1900s. Fed by the 68th St. and Dunne cribs; largely superseded operationally by the Sawyer plant's own intakes. Not to be confused with the offshore 68th Street Crib.

name: Lexington Pumping Station
note: 5555 W. Lexington St. Completed ~1990-92, built to bedrock (90 ft deep, 36-month construction) jointly with the DuPage Water Commission; delivers water 9.3 mi west to the DuPage Pumping Station in Elmhurst.

name: Cermak Pumping Station
note: 735 W. Harrison St. Built 1934-35 as the "Central Pumping Station" (fully electric from the outset, six 50-MGD pumps) to replace the obsolete 22nd St., Harrison St. and 14th St. stations; financed by a $2.327M RFC loan. Renamed Cermak.

name: Lakeview Pumping Station
note: 745 W. Wilson Ave. DWM's current facility list shows an active station at this address, but the original/historic Clarendon & Montrose Lake View Pumping Station (in service since 1875) was placed on standby 1964, retired 1965, and demolished 1978-79 (HAER IL-4). Whether/when a replacement was built at the Wilson Ave. address specifically is an open question in the source doc; 1965 used here as a placeholder pending confirmation.

name: Southwest Pumping Station
note: 8422 S. Kedvale Ave. Construction date not confirmed in any source reviewed (built sometime 1933-2009; 1955 used here as an undocumented rough midpoint estimate). Now the Chicago-side connection point for the Chicago-Grand Prairie Water Commission regional pipeline (2024-25 groundbreaking).

name: North Branch (Lawrence Avenue) Pumping Station
note: 4860 N. Francisco Ave., on the North Branch of the Chicago River just north of the Lawrence Ave. bridge. Art Deco building, 1928-30 (predecessor inland station 1907-1930, see mwrd-lawrence-original-ps). Five 300-cfs storm pumps + dry-weather lift to O'Brien WRP. Active; rehab contract 22-094-3D running through Aug. 2027.

name: Original Lawrence Avenue Sewage Pumping Station (1907-1930, superseded)
note: Inland predecessor to the riverside North Branch PS, sited at Lawrence Ave. and the C.M.&St.P.R.R./Northwestern Elevated crossing, east of old Evanston Ave. (renamed Broadway 1913) -- approximate position near the Lawrence/Broadway intersection in Uptown; exact parcel and the building's post-1930 fate are undocumented. Built by the City of Chicago; lifted North Side sewage into the 16-ft Lawrence Ave. conduit and forced lake water through to flush the North Branch. Abandoned upon the 1930 completion of the riverside station.

name: 39th Street Sewage Pumping Station (1907, South Side)
note: South Side counterpart to the original Lawrence Avenue PS, completed the same year (1907) as part of the City/Sanitary District intercepting-sewer agreement, lifting sewage into the 20-ft 39th St. conduit toward a lakefront outfall. Approximate position at the pre-landfill-era shoreline near 39th St. (Burnham Park later filled this area substantially east); the 1930 end-date is inferred by analogy to the Lawrence Ave. station's documented supersession and is not independently confirmed.

name: Racine Avenue Pumping Station (RAPS)
note: Racine Ave. near Pershing Rd., Bridgeport. Main South Side sewage pumps conveying flow to Stickney WRP; 3,125 cfs storm capacity. Sources disagree whether a distinct, larger 1964 rebuild replaced the 1939 structure. Green roof retrofit completed 2015.

name: 95th Street Pumping Station
note: Baltimore Ave. near the Calumet River. Built Apr.-Jun. 1924, operational 1925; 855 cfs storm capacity to the CWS. Powered originally by motor-generator sets with coal delivered by a dedicated rail spur.

name: 122nd Street Pumping Station
note: Calumet River, Hegewisch. 375 cfs storm capacity to the CWS. Construction date not documented in sources reviewed; 1925 used here as an inferred estimate matching the adjacent, well-documented 95th St. PS's 1924-25 construction era.

name: 125th Street Pumping Station
note: 125th St. & Michigan Ave. area, discharging to the Little Calumet River; 1,140 cfs storm capacity. Construction date not documented (1925 is an inferred estimate by analogy to 95th/122nd St.). Coordinates computed from Chicago's street-address grid (Nominatim geocoding was rate-limited during research) -- treat as approximate. Boiler/energy retrofit completed 2017.

name: Westchester Pumping Station
note: MWRD internal facility #150 ("WCPS"), 1140 Gardner Rd., Westchester, IL 60154. Built 1951 to relieve the Berkley-Hillside and Broadview-Bellwood Intercepting Sewers; discharges CSO to Addison Creek (Outfall 150, TARP structure DS-D34-AI). Dry-weather flow continues to Stickney WRP. Coordinate geocoded (Nominatim) from the primary-source street address given in MWRD's Contract 21-168-3S (Westchester Pumping Station Relief Sewer) SRF project notification.

name: TARP Mainstream Pumping Station
note: 6100 River Rd., Hodgkins. Dewaters the Mainstream Tunnel System and McCook Reservoir to Stickney WRP; largest pump 17,500 hp, lifts water 300 ft. Pumps in service since May 1985; $23.38M pump-overhaul contract awarded 2021.

name: Calumet TARP Pumping Station
note: 400 E. 130th St., Riverdale (Doty Ave. area, on the Calumet WRP site). Pump-room floor ~365 ft below grade -- the lowest inhabited point in Chicago. Dewaters the Calumet Tunnel System/Thornton Reservoir to Calumet WRP. Built under 1970s-80s contract 74-206-2H; coordinate offset slightly from the adjacent Calumet WRP marker.

name: Stickney Water Reclamation Plant
note: 6001 W. Pershing Rd., Cicero. Largest MWRD plant, largest wastewater plant in the world by treatment volume. Opened as the West Side works (1930) alongside the Southwest works (1939); combined and renamed Stickney in 1988. Design avg. flow 1,200 MGD.

name: Calumet Water Reclamation Plant
note: 400 E. 130th St. MWRD's oldest continuously operating treatment works, in service since 1922 (activated-sludge units 1923). Serves Chicago's South Side and 48 south Cook Co. suburbs; receives Thornton Reservoir's stored combined sewage for treatment.

name: Terrence J. O'Brien Water Reclamation Plant
note: 3500 Howard St., Skokie. Built as the North Side Sewage Treatment Works, contract let 1923, operating Oct. 3, 1928; renamed 2012 for longtime MWRD Board President Terrence J. O'Brien. Fed by the North Branch Pumping Station; UV disinfection since 2016.

name: James C. Kirie Water Reclamation Plant
note: 701 W. Oakton St., Des Plaines. Opened May 12, 1980, MWRD's newest and last-built of the seven WRPs; a "no digestion" satellite plant piping solids to Egan WRP. Fed by the Upper Des Plaines TARP system/Majewski Reservoir.

name: John E. Egan Water Reclamation Plant
note: 550 S. Meacham Rd., Schaumburg. Operational Dec. 16, 1975, formally dedicated June 4, 1976 (bicentennial year); named for MWRD Board President John E. Egan. Hub of the suburban solids-handling chain, digesting sludge from Kirie as well as its own.

name: Hanover Park Water Reclamation Plant
note: 1200 Sycamore Ave., Hanover Park. Operational July 19, 1963, the earliest of the four northwest suburban satellites. Site of President Nixon's Feb. 6, 1970 inspection tour; essentially all biosolids consumed on-site via the adjacent 200-acre Fischer Farm.

name: Lemont Water Reclamation Plant
note: 13 Stephen St., Lemont. Operational Nov. 1961, the smallest of MWRD's seven WRPs, serving only the Village of Lemont; discharges directly to the Chicago Sanitary and Ship Canal. New Wet Weather Treatment Facility/5-MG reservoir completed 2013-2018.

name: Des Plaines River Sewage Treatment Works (Maywood, predecessor/experimental plant)
note: Early (1920-1931) Sanitary District experimental sewage-works site on the Des Plaines River near Maywood, IL -- a process-testing predecessor distinct from the modern suburban satellite-plant program (not a direct predecessor of Kirie/Egan/Hanover Park/Lemont). Since demolished; site converted to a Forest Preserve District maintenance yard. No precise coordinates were located in sources reviewed; position is an approximate placement near the Des Plaines River at Maywood.

name: Gloria Alitto Majewski Reservoir
note: Elk Grove Village, adjacent to Kirie WRP. First TARP Phase II/CUP reservoir, 0.35 BG (350 MG), completed 1998 for ~$45M; originally the "O'Hare Reservoir," renamed for MWRD Commissioner Gloria Alitto Majewski. Fed by the Upper Des Plaines Tunnel System.

name: Thornton Composite Reservoir
note: North lobe of the Thornton Quarry, South Holland/Thornton, IL. 7.9 BG total (4.8 BG CSO/TARP + 3.1 BG Thorn Creek overbank); excavation complete 2013, operational end of 2015 (dedicated Sept. 1, 2015). Fed by the Calumet Tunnel System; drains to Calumet WRP.

name: Thornton Transitional Reservoir (decommissioned)
note: West lobe of the Thornton Quarry. ~3.1 BG temporary NRCS flood-control basin, operational March 2003, decommissioned Sept. 2022 after the permanent Composite Reservoir and Thorn Creek Diversion/Overflow Tunnels came online (58+ BG captured in 83 fill events over its life).

name: McCook Reservoir (Stage 1)
note: Bedford Park/McCook, IL, between the Chicago Sanitary & Ship Canal and the Des Plaines River (former Vulcan Materials quarry site). Stage 1 (3.5 BG) mining complete 2016, operational end of 2017; Stage 2 (+6.5 BG, target 10 BG total) ~72% excavated as of mid-2025, revised completion Dec. 31, 2032. Dewaters via the Mainstream Pumping Station to Stickney WRP. Coordinate is an approximate quarry-area centroid (precise excavation-boundary coordinates were not located in sources reviewed).

name: Chicago Harbor Lock & Chicago River Controlling Works
note: Mouth of the Chicago River at Lake Michigan, near Navy Pier. Built 1936-38; regulates the boundary between the Chicago Area Waterway System and Lake Michigan, one of the two busiest locks in the U.S. Three consecutive winters (2023-26) of major structural rehab.

name: Thomas J. O'Brien Lock and Dam
note: Calumet River, Hegewisch (near 130th St.). Built 1957-1965 (task brief cites 1960), replacing the Sanitary District's older Blue Island Lock; controls the Calumet River's connection to Lake Michigan.

name: Blue Island Lock (predecessor to O'Brien Lock, removed)
note: Sanitary District lock near Blue Island, IL on the Cal-Sag Channel/Little Calumet corridor, superseded when the USACE's T.J. O'Brien Lock & Dam was built 1957-1965 on the Calumet River. No precise former-site coordinate was located in sources reviewed; position is an approximate placement near Blue Island.

name: Wilmette Pumping Station (North Shore Channel controlling works)
note: Beneath the Sheridan Rd. bridge, Wilmette. Built 1908-1910 as the North Shore Channel's lake-diversion controlling structure (lock added 1912); 4 horizontal screw pumps, 1,000 cfs rated (only 2 of 4 original pumps still in service). $17.5M gate/pump renovation 2012.

name: Lockport Powerhouse
note: Sanitary and Ship Canal at Lockport, IL. Hydroelectric plant energized Nov. 26, 1907 (Isham Randolph/G.M. Wisner engineering, Hayes Brothers construction); oldest operating hydro plant in Illinois, still generating (2 modern Kaplan units installed ~2000). Discharges the entire CAWS flow into the Des Plaines River above Joliet.

name: Lockport Controlling Works
note: Sanitary and Ship Canal at Lockport, IL, immediately adjacent to the Powerhouse. Built 1895-99 (bear-trap dam + 7 Stoney sluice gates); bear-trap dam removed 1938 (WPA project), sluice gates still operating. Coordinate offset ~200m from the geocoded Powerhouse position; exact structure-specific coordinate not independently confirmed.

name: Lockport Lock (USACE, Illinois Waterway)
note: Sanitary and Ship Canal at Lockport, IL, just downstream of the Controlling Works/Powerhouse. Current USACE lock (110x600 ft, ~40 ft lift, highest on the Illinois Waterway) opened 1933, superseding the original 1907-1910/1933 Sanitary District lock (22x130 ft). Major rehab completed 2017, $150.3M. Coordinate offset from the Powerhouse position; exact lock-chamber coordinate not independently confirmed.

name: North Branch Dam (removed)
note: River Park, south of Foster Ave., where the North Shore Channel met the natural North Branch of the Chicago River (river mile 333.3). Low-head concrete dam (single 4-ft drop) built 1910 to hold the channel's lower, canal-controlled elevation apart from the natural upper river; removed starting July 31, 2018 by USACE/MWRD/Chicago Park District, replaced with a graded riffle-pool channel. Coordinate is the River Park centroid, not the precise former dam site.

name: Bridgeport I&M Canal Pumping Works (historic, removed)
note: Ashland Ave. area at the Illinois & Michigan Canal/South Branch, Bridgeport. Steam pumps lifting ~100 cfs from the South Branch into the I&M Canal's summit level to keep it navigable and to flush sewage (extra 45 days/yr run 1861-1870); rendered largely unnecessary once the 1871 "deep cut" allowed gravity flow, though some source material implies continued/backup use into the early 1900s. No precise site coordinate located; position computed from Chicago's street-address grid near Ashland Ave. and the historic canal alignment.

name: Fullerton Avenue Conduit Pumping Station (historic, likely removed)
note: Fullerton Ave. at the North Branch of the Chicago River. Mid-route lift on the lake-to-river Fullerton Ave. flushing conduit (predates the Lawrence Ave. conduit); an 1911 engineering paper already suggested it be abandoned once the newer Lawrence Ave. conduit opened. No precise site coordinate or confirmed removal date located; position computed from Chicago's street-address grid.

name: Electric Dispersal Barrier System
note: Chicago Sanitary and Ship Canal near Romeoville, IL (~477 ft upstream of the Romeo Rd./135th St. bridge). USACE electric barrier system deterring Asian carp and other invasive species from moving between the Great Lakes and Mississippi River basins. Coordinate is a USGS monitoring point near the barrier, not its precise official centerline.

name: Lawrence Avenue Conduit Lake Intake (historic)
note: Near Sheridan Rd., Uptown lakefront -- the lake (east) end of the 16-ft Lawrence Ave. conduit whose inland pumping station stood miles west at Lawrence/Broadway. Fed a low-head screw pump forcing lake water down the conduit to flush the North Branch; this flushing function became redundant once the North Shore Channel/Wilmette PS opened in 1910. Position is approximate -- an 1901 Sanitary District exhibit uses "Sheridan Road" only as a calculation datum, not a structure coordinate.

name: 39th Street Conduit Lake Intake (historic)
note: Lakefront at 39th St. (pre-landfill-era shoreline, well west of today's Burnham Park shoreline). Lake end of the 20-ft 39th St. conduit paired with the original 39th Street sewage pumping station. No precise site coordinate located; position computed from Chicago's street-address grid at the approximate historic shoreline.
